item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what happens if you put SCP-096 in it?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that the image appears to be from here, with no idication it has been released under a license compliant with the wiki's (CC BY-SA 3.0). Thus, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, I have an idea for you, if you're up for writing it.

What if they used a robotic arm to open the door?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
look, it's a me!

If I ever encounter a clone/copy of myself, I am using that phrase.

For the record, I found the SCP itself pretty scary/interesting, but I don't think an effective write-up of Harrison's log is possible, for the same reasons final logs for 610 and 087 will never be published.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the incident logs incredibly difficult to believe. The idea that people can be so easily driven to kill apparent duplicates of themselves just by being presented with them is not at all realistic. I'd expect the gang-homicide incident where they said "hay" and cooperated to be more prevalent than "look, it's a me! I'll violently and slowly kill you, me, nyaaargh!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very cool! Personally sounds fun being able to /meet/ yourself… Apart from the small "having to kill yourself to leave" detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if both of them die at the same time i.e. they both starved to death at the same time

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I assume instead of having the normal circumstance of having a "mirror" of the room (floor plan reversed) it's two identical rooms.

The connecting door must be closed before the main door can be opened, as an experiment occurs to me; go into Room A, close that door and kill your duplicate. I'd *think* the external observers would be able to open the door to an empty room.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This makes me think of this article:

http://www.cracked.com/blog/human-clones-do-you-fk-or-fight/

Since the survivor has to tell his/her own story, we may never know if any duplicates tried Option 1 before Option 2…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, but I'm withholding an upvote just because a few things weren't really clear to me until I hit almost the bottom of the article.

The whole "cloning" effect should really be described so that it's clear that that's what it's doing in the description. I was confused about this until I hit the incident log.

In incident 3, it talks about them being trapped in there for weeks. Why didn't they just close the door and leave? I would be sufficiently creeped the fuck out by seeing my clone and my wife's clone after about 3 seconds.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was somewhat meh!… Then I read Doctor Harrison's log. It's a room that is helping you deal with your issues by sort of "If you meet Budha on the road, kill him". If you send the guy without issues, he just stays there and both of him keep living. Awesome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The windows on the far wall of the bedroom cannot be opened and the walls, floor, and ceiling cannot be damaged or breached by any known means.

Vague and it just seems like lazy writing to make sure people can't escape.

Overall it's just not interesting. It reminds me of a boring hotel room version of a test in the movie Saw and I found that the Incident Logs are mostly redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A suicide you survive. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent. A room that drives you to suicide. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only one fridge was generating food. If that's unclear I can try to clarify it a bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Question. In the incident with the duplicated couple, only one of the two fridges is reported as generating sandwiches. Is this an oversight or was the food consistently generated only by the fridge on the "losing" side?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There technically is no original and no duplicate, but the naming scheme gives the survivor the -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the "original" one always the one that survives in the logs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me a lot of No Exit by Jean-Paul Sartre.

I'd love to an exploration of what counts as dead or what counts as one person to this SCP. There are edge cases, and access to other SCPs makes them more common.
—-

I mean, what happens if a drone opens the door? What's death: Heart-stopping or brain-death, or perhaps some more mysterious sign?
—-

If a part of someone's mind or personality has been removed, what happen? If you don't want to refer to an existing SCP, you can just redact the SCP number and it will be fine as a standalone.
—-
Of course, no testing on immortals allowed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to comment on the last test log feeling incomplete, but that's because it is. I do look forward to seeing it, but in the meantime you've crafted a very concise and well-contained sort of thing here, with excellent atmosphere. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote, and I'm definitely looking forward to that document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well she wasn't exactly cheating on her husband, she was cheating with her husband.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like this one, and am glad it doesn't derail my own draft for a "door to a mirror world" SCP I'm working on (no it's nothing like this, the phrase "mirror" being a bit literal in my case)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More like SCP: Beyond Thunderdome.

One man enters, becomes two men, one man leaves!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One enters. One materializes. Two fight. One leaves.

The SCP Games

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having read the trilogy, I disagree. Or at least, this is sufficiently different that it doesn't trigger that association for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neither have I, but I think it still applies. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh. I've never even read those books but I think I get what you're saying. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. Feels too much like the Hunger Games during testing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll definitely post it as a draft to get some reactions first. If people think it takes away from the mystery I can just modify the testing log a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed w/ Bright to not include Harrison's log. For one thing, how would the Foundation have access to it, since he's still in the session?

Maybe as a Tale, but even then I'd wait a while before posting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoa, it took me to like the second incident report to figure out they had to murder their counterpart to leave. Blew my mind, great article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You expanded on it like I advised. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. +1

I strongly advise AGAINST writing out Harrisons log, as that might take away some of the interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting concept. It was a little hard to understand at first, but I attribute it mostly to the confusing subject matter and less to poor wording. Reading the logs helped me understand a lot easier, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For now. It will be a supplemental document, hopefully completed sometime this weekend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Harrison's seems incomplete. Intentional?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, this makes more sense to me. I was seeing the tensions generated as a representation of their sins (infidelity, etc) while they were really just caused to produce hostility. Thanks for clearing that up, I think I will upboat this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I'm hoping to have Dr. Harrison's log done this weekend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The incident logs are disturbingly amusing, particularly the one with the married couple. I like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me it feels a lot like a "Tension Generator", what with the creature comforts of the room shifting and fluctuating to create friction between duplicates. I don't see anything about specifically creating one's "sins" other than that they're duplicated along with every other element of one's personality… and it functions more like a Deathmatch cage than anything else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really lovin this idea, dude. The incident logs really put it into upvote category for me. I really want to see this interview log, too. I feel like it NEEEDS it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting experiment. At that point it would take a lot of prior convincing to get that person to open the door again. :) It's not like he can be forced to do so without trapping someone else in there with him.

Also, is it malice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice. My only qualm with this is the implicit malice with the diminishing sandwiches.

One thing I'd like to see tested is putting someone in this over and over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct. I'll try to clarify to make that more clear, but the English language is ill-suited for describing this kind of thing. Maybe "duplicate" is throwing people off. Hmm…

The door won't open until the dead one is definitively dead. No resuscitation would be possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All good questions. I'll attempt to clarify.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's really symmetrical, isn't it? It's not an original and a duplicate, it's two of the original. When one dies and the door opens, the survivor gets treated as the "original" and the dead one as the "duplicate" because the survivor obviously remembers being the original. But so did the dead one, before they were killed.

(If the door was opened and a medical team got in quickly enough after the death, could it be possible to resuscitate the "duplicate"?)

I like it. Have an upvote. I was going to give you two, but one of them got killed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The windows on the far wall of the bedroom cannot be opened or broken by any known means.

I assume this applies to the walls, floor and ceiling? Since otherwise a spy-hole could be drilled through a surface to try to look inside while it's active.

When viewed from through the windows from outside SCP-1197 appears empty and undisturbed despite any occupants.

Always, or only when the room is active?

Opening SCP-1197-1 will reveal an identical version of SCP-1197 and a duplicate of any current occupants, who have opened their version of SCP-1197-1 at the same moment.

Can SCP-1197-1 only be opened when the door to the hallway is closed, or does opening SCP-1197-1 cause the hallway door to slam shut? If the later, and (for example) the hallway door was jammed open with a steel girder, would the hallway door slam shut in a manner which cuts through the girder like it was made from butter?

Should 1 instance of each occupant expire, SCP-1197-1 will close and the entrance door will open.

If a subject enters the room, opens SCP-1197-1, crosses into the duplicate room and kills the duplicate, will the entrance door open onto the duplicate room, with the original room vanishing? If the subject remains in the original room and kills the duplicate while the duplicate is still in the duplicate room (like with a gun), will the corpse of the duplicate vanish, or will it teleport into the original room?

Subject described meeting his duplicate and exchanging questions until both accepted their situation.

Do recording devices not work while the room is active, forcing the foundation to interview the subjects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you be more specific?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. This is probably something different, but this is what I get out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm so close to upvoting this but the last paragraph of the description is too vague.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok. Perhaps Legendary is right and I should write up Dr. Harrison's log sooner rather than later. It would probably clear that up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No…

What I'm saying is what I see this article as. When they open it, they come face to face with their problems, then have to confront them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's still not what you're describing. Opening SCP-1197-1 duplicates the room and its occupants perfectly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's why I said "YOUR" worst sins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a parallel dimension and there's no sins there except the ones you bring with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may be missing something, but all I get out of this is "Window to a parallel dimension where your worst sins lie!"

To me, this just seems like a cross between 093 and 666, put in a hotel room.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would recommend putting them all under one collapsible under "SCP-1197 Incident Log" or something. Having such short excerpts under separate collapsibles right now is a bit too cluttered, in my opinion.
